comment_text.sn35.95:1.1: Māluṅkyaputta was, according to Pali commentaries, the son of the lady Māluṅkyā of Kosala. Alternatively, Sanskrit dictionaries give māluka as the name of a people or a mixed caste, although I have not been able to trace any further details. His conversion is not recorded as he appears always as a monk. MN 63 and MN 64 depict him as somewhat of a fool, but it seems that he persisted, and late in life sought teachings in meditation and ultimately realized arahantship, of which we have two different accounts (here and AN 4.257). The latter contains an extensive set of verses attributed to him, which are repeated at Thag 16.5. A second set of his verses are at Thag 6.5.
comment_text.sn35.95:4.2: The commentary explains “unseen, unseen before” as not seen in this life or past lives. This is possible, although adiṭṭhapubba (and its parallels) are elsewhere used for the simple past in this life (eg. DN 16:2.17.3, SN 35.245:6.1; assutapubba at MN 12:50.6).
comment_text.sn35.95:10.1: For the “epistemic tetrad”, see MN 1:19.1. This teaching is famous from the brief instructions for Bāhiya at Ud 1.10:8.2 | This set of four overlaps but does not map neatly on to the six senses, which lack an equivalent for “thought”; this is the only place in the Suttas that connects them. Rather, they emphasize the means by which spiritual truths are known. The missing items smell, taste, and touch are not normally means of learning spiritual truths, unlike the “seen” (a holy person, a dream, etc.), the “heard” (the words of a teacher or oral tradition), the “thought” (philosophical contemplation), and the “known” (reflective or meditative understanding). | The expression “merely” (matta) suggests that these things are seen simply as they are, without being proliferated by conceit, or assumed to be absolute sources of truth.
comment_text.sn35.95:10.3: Whereas the six senses primarily stimulate an emotional response of desire, the epistemic tetrad is concerned with the creation of self-identity, responding to the Upaniṣadic idea that these are the means by which the Self is realized. | “By that” suggests that one assumes a Self from the tetrad, eg. by hearing about it, or deducing it from reasoning, etc. The instrumental case draws on Yājñavalkya: “By the seeing, hearing, thinking, and knowing of the Self, all this is known” (ātmano vā are darśanena śravaṇena matyā vijñānenedaṁ sarvaṁ viditam, Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.4.5).
comment_text.sn35.95:10.5: Once the Self has been derived from the tetrad, one conceives Self in relation to it. Specifically, this rejects the imperishable Self posited by Yājñavalkya, which sees but cannot be seen. This again echoes Yājñavalkya, in a variant passage of the previous, although he is using the locative case in an absolute sense: “When the Self is seen, heard, thought, and known, all this is known.” (ātmani khalv are dṛṣṭe śrute mate vijñāta idaṁ sarvaṁ viditam, Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 4.5.6).

root_text.sn35.95:1.1: Atha kho āyasmā mālukyaputto yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami …pe… ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā mālukyaputto bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn35.95:1.2: “sādhu me, bhante, bhagavā saṅkhittena dhammaṁ desetu, yamahaṁ bhagavato dhammaṁ sutvā eko vūpakaṭṭho appamatto ātāpī pahitatto vihareyyan”ti.
root_text.sn35.95:2.1: “Ettha dāni, mālukyaputta, kiṁ dahare bhikkhū vakkhāma.
root_text.sn35.95:2.2: Yatra hi nāma tvaṁ, bhikkhu, jiṇṇo vuddho mahallako addhagato vayoanuppatto saṅkhittena ovādaṁ yācasī”ti.
root_text.sn35.95:3.1: “Kiñcāpāhaṁ, bhante, jiṇṇo vuddho mahallako addhagato vayoanuppatto.
root_text.sn35.95:3.2: Desetu me, bhante, bhagavā saṅkhittena dhammaṁ, desetu sugato saṅkhittena dhammaṁ, appeva nāmāhaṁ bhagavato bhāsitassa atthaṁ ājāneyyaṁ. Appeva nāmāhaṁ bhagavato bhāsitassa dāyādo assan”ti.
root_text.sn35.95:4.2: ye te cakkhuviññeyyā rūpā adiṭṭhā adiṭṭhapubbā, na ca passasi, na ca te hoti passeyyanti? Atthi te tattha chando vā rāgo vā pemaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn35.95:5.1: “Ye te sotaviññeyyā saddā assutā assutapubbā, na ca suṇāsi, na ca te hoti suṇeyyanti? Atthi te tattha chando vā rāgo vā pemaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn35.95:6.1: “Ye te ghānaviññeyyā gandhā aghāyitā aghāyitapubbā, na ca ghāyasi, na ca te hoti ghāyeyyanti? Atthi te tattha chando vā rāgo vā pemaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn35.95:7.1: “Ye te jivhāviññeyyā rasā asāyitā asāyitapubbā, na ca sāyasi, na ca te hoti sāyeyyanti? Atthi te tattha chando vā rāgo vā pemaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn35.95:8.1: “Ye te kāyaviññeyyā phoṭṭhabbā asamphuṭṭhā asamphuṭṭhapubbā, na ca phusasi, na ca te hoti phuseyyanti? Atthi te tattha chando vā rāgo vā pemaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn35.95:9.1: “Ye te manoviññeyyā dhammā aviññātā aviññātapubbā, na ca vijānāsi, na ca te hoti vijāneyyanti? Atthi te tattha chando vā rāgo vā pemaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn35.95:10.1: “Ettha ca te, mālukyaputta, diṭṭhasutamutaviññātabbesu dhammesu diṭṭhe diṭṭhamattaṁ bhavissati, sute sutamattaṁ bhavissati, mute mutamattaṁ bhavissati, viññāte viññātamattaṁ bhavissati.
root_text.sn35.95:10.2: Yato kho te, mālukyaputta, diṭṭhasutamutaviññātabbesu dhammesu diṭṭhe diṭṭhamattaṁ bhavissati, sute sutamattaṁ bhavissati, mute mutamattaṁ bhavissati, viññāte viññātamattaṁ bhavissati;
root_text.sn35.95:10.7: tato tvaṁ, mālukyaputta, nevidha, na huraṁ, na ubhayamantarena.
root_text.sn35.95:11.1: “Imassa khvāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavatā saṅkhittena bhāsitassa vitthārena atthaṁ ājānāmi:
root_text.sn35.95:36.1: Imassa khvāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavatā saṅkhittena bhāsitassa evaṁ vitthārena atthaṁ ājānāmī”ti.
root_text.sn35.95:36.3: Sādhu kho tvaṁ, mālukyaputta, mayā saṅkhittena bhāsitassa vitthārena atthaṁ ājānāsi:
root_text.sn35.95:41.1: Imassa kho, mālukyaputta, mayā saṅkhittena bhāsitassa evaṁ vitthārena attho daṭṭhabbo”ti.
root_text.sn35.95:42.1: Atha kho āyasmā mālukyaputto bhagavato bhāsitaṁ abhinanditvā anumoditvā uṭṭhāyāsanā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā padakkhiṇaṁ katvā pakkāmi.
root_text.sn35.95:42.2: Atha kho āyasmā mālukyaputto eko vūpakaṭṭho appamatto ātāpī pahitatto viharanto nacirasseva—yassatthāya kulaputtā sammadeva agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajanti, tadanuttaraṁ—brahmacariyapariyosānaṁ diṭṭheva dhamme sayaṁ abhiññā sacchikatvā upasampajja vihāsi.
root_text.sn35.95:42.3: “Khīṇā jāti, vusitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, kataṁ karaṇīyaṁ, nāparaṁ itthattāyā”ti abbhaññāsi.
root_text.sn35.95:42.4: Aññataro ca panāyasmā mālukyaputto arahataṁ ahosīti.

translation_text.sn35.95:1.1: Then Venerable Māluṅkyaputta went up to the Buddha … and asked him,
translation_text.sn35.95:1.2: “Sir, may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief. When I’ve heard it, I’ll live alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute.”
translation_text.sn35.95:2.1: “Well now, Māluṅkyaputta, what are we to say to the young monks,
translation_text.sn35.95:2.2: when even an old man like you, elderly and senior, advanced in years, having reached the final stage of life, asks for brief advice?”
translation_text.sn35.95:3.1: “Sir, even though I’m an old man, elderly and senior,
translation_text.sn35.95:3.2: may the Buddha please teach me Dhamma in brief! May the Holy one please teach me in brief! Hopefully I can understand the meaning of what the Buddha says. Hopefully I can be an heir of the Buddha’s teaching!”
translation_text.sn35.95:4.2: Do you have any desire or greed or fondness for sights known by the eye that are unseen, unseen before, and which you neither see nor think might be seen?”
translation_text.sn35.95:5.1: “Do you have any desire or greed or affection for sounds known by the ear …
translation_text.sn35.95:9.1: ideas known by the mind that are unknown, unknown before, and which you neither know nor think might be known?”
translation_text.sn35.95:10.1: “In that case, when it comes to things that ought be seen, heard, thought, and known: in the seen will be merely the seen; in the heard will be merely the heard; in the thought will be merely the thought; in the known will be merely the known.
translation_text.sn35.95:10.7: you won’t be in this life or the next or in between the two.
translation_text.sn35.95:10.8: Just this is the end of suffering.”
translation_text.sn35.95:11.1: “This is how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement:
translation_text.sn35.95:12.1: ‘When you see a sight, mindfulness is lost
translation_text.sn35.95:12.2: as you focus on a pleasant feature.
translation_text.sn35.95:12.3: Experiencing it with a mind full of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:13.6: you’re said to be far from extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:14.1: When you hear a sound, mindfulness is lost
translation_text.sn35.95:14.2: as you focus on a pleasant feature.
translation_text.sn35.95:14.3: Experiencing it with a mind full of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:15.6: you’re said to be far from extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:16.1: When you smell an odor, mindfulness is lost
translation_text.sn35.95:16.2: as you focus on a pleasant feature.
translation_text.sn35.95:16.3: Experiencing it with a mind full of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:17.6: you’re said to be far from extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:18.1: When you enjoy a taste, mindfulness is lost
translation_text.sn35.95:18.2: as you focus on a pleasant feature.
translation_text.sn35.95:18.3: Experiencing it with a mind full of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:19.6: you’re said to be far from extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:20.1: When you sense a touch, mindfulness is lost
translation_text.sn35.95:20.2: as you focus on a pleasant feature.
translation_text.sn35.95:20.3: Experiencing it with a mind full of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:21.6: you’re said to be far from extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:22.1: When you know an idea, mindfulness is lost
translation_text.sn35.95:22.2: as you focus on a pleasant feature.
translation_text.sn35.95:22.3: Experiencing it with a mind full of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:23.6: you’re said to be far from extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:24.2: when you see a sight with mindfulness.
translation_text.sn35.95:24.3: Experiencing it with a mind free of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:24.4: you don’t keep clinging to it.
translation_text.sn35.95:25.1: Even as you see a sight
translation_text.sn35.95:25.2: and get familiar with how it feels,
translation_text.sn35.95:25.3: you wear away, you don’t heap up:
translation_text.sn35.95:25.6: you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:26.2: when you hear a sound with mindfulness.
translation_text.sn35.95:26.3: Experiencing it with a mind free of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:26.4: you don’t keep clinging to it.
translation_text.sn35.95:27.1: Even as you hear a sound
translation_text.sn35.95:27.2: and get familiar with how it feels,
translation_text.sn35.95:27.3: you wear away, you don’t heap up:
translation_text.sn35.95:27.6: you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:28.2: when you smell an odor with mindfulness.
translation_text.sn35.95:28.3: Experiencing it with a mind free of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:28.4: you don’t keep clinging to it.
translation_text.sn35.95:29.1: Even as you smell an odor
translation_text.sn35.95:29.2: and get familiar with how it feels,
translation_text.sn35.95:29.3: you wear away, you don’t heap up:
translation_text.sn35.95:29.6: you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:30.2: when you enjoy a taste with mindfulness.
translation_text.sn35.95:30.3: Experiencing it with a mind free of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:30.4: you don’t keep clinging to it.
translation_text.sn35.95:31.1: Even as you savor a taste
translation_text.sn35.95:31.2: and get familiar with how it feels,
translation_text.sn35.95:31.3: you wear away, you don’t heap up:
translation_text.sn35.95:31.6: you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:32.2: when you sense a touch with mindfulness.
translation_text.sn35.95:32.3: Experiencing it with a mind free of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:32.4: you don’t keep clinging to it.
translation_text.sn35.95:33.1: Even as you sense a touch
translation_text.sn35.95:33.2: and get familiar with how it feels,
translation_text.sn35.95:33.3: you wear away, you don’t heap up:
translation_text.sn35.95:33.6: you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment.
translation_text.sn35.95:34.2: when you know an idea with mindfulness.
translation_text.sn35.95:34.3: Experiencing it with a mind free of desire,
translation_text.sn35.95:34.4: you don’t keep clinging to it.
translation_text.sn35.95:35.1: Even as you know an idea
translation_text.sn35.95:35.2: and get familiar with how it feels,
translation_text.sn35.95:35.3: you wear away, you don’t heap up:
translation_text.sn35.95:35.6: you’re said to be in the presence of extinguishment.’
translation_text.sn35.95:36.1: That’s how I understand the detailed meaning of the Buddha’s brief statement.”
translation_text.sn35.95:36.3: It’s good that you understand the detailed meaning of what I’ve said in brief like this.” And he repeated the verses in full.
translation_text.sn35.95:41.1: “This is how to understand the detailed meaning of what I said in brief.”
translation_text.sn35.95:42.1: And then Venerable Māluṅkyaputta approved and agreed with what the Buddha said. He got up from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled the Buddha, keeping him on his right, before leaving.
translation_text.sn35.95:42.2: Then Māluṅkyaputta, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. He lived having achieved with his own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.
translation_text.sn35.95:42.3: He understood: “Rebirth is ended; the spiritual journey has been completed; what had to be done has been done; there is nothing further for this place.”
translation_text.sn35.95:42.4: And Venerable Māluṅkyaputta became one of the perfected.
